Tweet by @trevin (Trevin Chow)
Author bio: Exploring, building and obsessing over AI. Ex: Big Cartel, Axon, Sketchy, Nike, Microsoft.
Text: https://t.co/g1N5dn6K7e
Created: 2026-03-31T08:17:03.000Z
Metrics: {"retweet_count":14,"reply_count":6,"like_count":158,"quote_count":5,"bookmark_count":305,"impression_count":47740}
Article content: {"plain_text":"A few releases have landed since the last update, with v2.60.0, landing today. The through-line is tightening the pipeline end-to-end: reviews are less noisy and no longer optional, plans catch more gaps before implementation, and the day-to-day friction of using the skills keeps going down.
If you're not using Compound Engineering already, what are you waiting for?!
 
[Updated] /ce:review — headless mode, mandatory invocation, and confidence scoring
This is where the biggest investment went across these releases. First, reviews got a headless mode so other skills can invoke them programmatically without interactive prompts or git ceremony. That unlocked the next step: making code review mandatory across the entire pipeline — ce:work, ce:brainstorm, ce:plan all now enforce review as a non-negotiable checkpoint instead of an optional step nobody triggered. ce:work presents full review as the default tier, with limited review requiring justification.
Then 2.60 layered on a confidence rubric that runs every finding through a 6-tier scoring system (0.00–1.00) with a 0.60 suppression threshold. Six specific false-positive categories get targeted: pre-existing issues, style nitpicks, intentional patterns, handled-elsewhere cases, code restatement, and generic advice. ~49% reduction in false positives without losing real bug detection. Reviews also verify intent against your PR context (title, body, linked issue) so findings that contradict what you're actually doing get suppressed. If two or more persona reviewers flag the same issue, confidence gets a 0.10 boost. Findings are now enforced as pipe-delimited tables instead of freeform prose, which makes them scannable and consistent. The resolve-base.sh script also got hardened for forked repos and shallow checkouts with retry logic and unshallowing fallback.
The net effect: reviews run more often automatically, but they're dramatically less noisy than before while applying important auto fixes. That's the right order of operations: mandate first, then reduce noise, otherwise we'd be mandating noise.
[Updated] /ce:work — bare prompts, test discovery, and branch naming
You no longer need a pre-built plan document to use ce:work.  We know many of you just wanna rip, so we're not gonna fight ya on that one :)
Submit a raw prompt to ce:work and it assesses complexity automatically: trivial work skips ceremony, medium work gets inline tasks, complex work recommends going through planning first. Paired with that, universal test discovery now finds existing tests before implementation starts so tests stay in sync with code changes. Per-task testing deliberation replaces the old binary "tests pass" checklist with "testing addressed" — forcing you to think about whether a behavioral change needs a test, not just whether existing tests still pass. The testing-reviewer got a new 5th check that flags behavioral code changes (new branches, state mutations, API changes) with zero corresponding test additions.
Also: ce:work now detects auto-generated worktree branch names like Claude's "worktree-printing-ruby-raven" and suggests renaming to something meaningful. Nice QOL if you're running parallel agents and end up with a pile of nonsense branch names in your PR list.
[Updated] /ce:brainstorm and /ce:plan — verification rigor and interactive deepening
Brainstorming had a subtle but nasty bug: Phase 1.1 conflated "don't do technical design" with "don't look at technical files," which meant agents couldn't verify infrastructure claims against actual source files. You'd end up with requirements docs stating "table X does not exist" as fact without anyone checking the schema. That's fixed — verification of current state (reading schemas, routes, configs) is now always permitted, while implementation decisions still defer to planning. Unverified claims must be labeled as assumptions, and Phase 3 finalization catches unverified absence claims before they propagate downstream.
On the planning side, plans now have an interactive deepening mode where you can accept, reject, or discuss each agent's findings before they get integrated — instead of everything auto-merging and you sorting through it after. 
For those that miss the separate "deepen-plan" skill, we've added support for invoking it via ce:plan.  You just say "deepen" (or derivations of that) and it auto-runs; if it's ambiguous, the skill will confirms with first on whether you wanna deepen. 
Two bug fixes closed a gap where document-review was getting skipped after deepening. That's fixed: document-review is now mandatory regardless of path because we've found it results in dramatically better docs. Plans also flag blank test scenarios on feature-bearing units as incomplete during validation to improve your outcomes.
[New] Conditional visual aids across requirements, plans, and PR descriptions
A cross-cutting feature that landed in ce:brainstorm, ce:plan, and git-commit-push-pr simultaneously. When your document hits certain complexity thresholds (5+ non-linear units, 3+ interacting surfaces, dense system-wide impact sections), it automatically generates flow diagrams, comparison tables, or relationship diagrams in mermaid or ASCII. PR descriptions use a higher trigger threshold since reviewers scan rather than study them.
[Updated] /resolve-pr-feedback — feedback clustering
Now detects when you're playing whack-a-mole with the PR feedback and resolve loop by looking for multiple thematically similar comments hitting the same code area. 
It clusters by concern category and spatial proximity. After 2 fix-verify cycles, remaining issues surface as "recurring patterns" instead of individual tickets. Added an actionability filter that strips out approvals, status badges, and wrapper text so you're not dispatching agents to "handle" a 👍 emoji as a comment.
[Updated] document-review — simplified and smarter
The 3-tier autofix decision tree (auto / batch_confirm / present) collapsed to two tiers (auto / present) by eliminating the intermediate batch_confirm step that users always accepted anyway. Obvious fixes apply silently, ambiguous cases still surface for review. The end-of-review message now also tells you what to actually do next based on document type: requirements → "create a plan with /ce:plan", plans → "implement with /ce:work."
[Updated] /ce:compound
Learnings now use a track-based schema: bug-track docs (build errors, test failures, runtime errors) keep the full diagnostic fields, while knowledge-track docs (best practices, workflow issues, DX gaps) get a lighter template. 
Also added a discoverability check ensuring docs/solutions/ is referenced in your AGENTS.md or CLAUDE.md so agents can find the knowledge store even when you're not directly using a Compound Engineering skill. 
...and the infrastructure / reliability layer
Model field normalization centralized into src/utils/model.ts — fixes Qwen incorrectly mapping sonnet to claude-sonnet instead of claude-sonnet-4-6, adds MiniMax provider prefix support. Six platforms handled consistently.
Argument-hint cleanup across all ce:* skills — removes confusing bracket syntax, clarifies defaults (I may change my mind on this in the future though...)
Smart output defaults criterion added to the CLI Agent Readiness Reviewer.
ce:plan decision matrix form for visualizing flag combinations, plus an "unchanged invariants" section to document blast radius boundaries.","preview_text":"A few releases have landed since the last update, with v2.60.0, landing today. The through-line is tightening the pipeline end-to-end: reviews are less noisy and no longer optional, plans catch more","entities":{"urls":[{"text":"resolve-base.sh"},{"text":"https://github.com/EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin"},{"text":"https://abs-0.twimg.com/emoji/v2/svg/1f44d.svg"}]},"cover_media":"3_2038890738537406464","title":"Compound Engineering: 3/31/2026"}
